coredata.dc:title: Iodine functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI) crosslinked whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND) for antibacterial application
coredata.dc:creator: Mukherjee, Sayan
Pandey, Anurag Kumar
Dogra, Nantu
Das, Bodhisatwa
Singh, Uday Kumar
Dhara, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: Whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND) were synthesized using the microwave irradiation method, and its amine-rich surface functionality was crosslinked with covalently bound Iodine functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI) to produce WCND-DHFI. The physicochemical characterization of both WCND and WCND-DHFI was performed and compared to comprehend the consequence of iodination on the characteristics of WCND. The suitability of CND in biological environments was evaluated through in vitro cytocompatibility and Chorioallantoic Membrane (CAM) assay, as well as a hemocompatibility study. WCND-DHFI has shown enhanced cell viability against WCND. Further, the antibacterial properties of both CNDs were studied against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial strains, representing an enhancement in antibacterial activity after DHFI crosslinking. WCND-DHFI has depicted a stable and prominent bacteriostatic activity for up to 6 h for both strains of bacteria. WCND-DHFI has denoted a 99.996% and 99.999% loss of bacterial viability for gram-positive and negative strains, respectively. Novel surface functionalization portrays an improvement in antibacterial activity. Transmission and scanning electron microscopy represent the cell wall rupturing by the WCND-DHFI, resulting in bacterial death. The ROS-mediated bacteriostatic mechanism of WCND-DHFI has been explored through assessing lipid peroxidation and protein oxidation assay. Moreover, the oxidative damage of DNA also has been explored. WCND-DHFI is performing as a promising cytocompatible and hemocompatible material for antibacterial applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iodine functionalized Carbon nanodots
2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHF)
Antimicrobial resistance
Surface functionalization
Antimicrobial carbon nanodot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Crosslinking of WCND with DHFI and ninhydrin assay
Bacteria cultivation and loss of bacterial cell viability assessment
In vitro cytotoxicity and CAM assay
Loss of Bacterial cell viability assessment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to express their gratitude to Central Research Facilities (CRF), IIT Kharagpur for providing the material characterizing facilities. The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. Soumen Das, Prof. Nilmani Sarkar, Dr. Gayatri Mukherjee, and Dr. Ritobrata Goswami for using their facilities. The authors would like to thank Trina Roy, Ragavi Rajasekaran, Atul Kumar Ojha, Krishnabrata Panda, Suman Mishra, Biswajit Biswas, Shagnik Chatterjee, Saikat Biswas, and Debangshu Mukherjee for their valuable input and support throughout the experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the bacteriostatic mechanism of CND by interaction with bacterial cells, and membrane insertion.

a) Schematic of whey protein derived CND (WCND) synthesis, b) Stepwise schematic diagram of chemical reaction of synthesizing DHF-I cross-linked WCND and its proposed structure (Drawn through Chem-draw software), c) CND synthesized from whey protein different time intervals (3 min, 5 min, and 7 min). Visual inspection both in normal light and under UV light.

a) FTIR data analysis of WCND, DHFI and WCND-DHFI, b) Raman spectroscopic analysis of WCND and WCND-DHFI, c) the full XPS spectrum of these materials and elemental identification, d) Deconvoluted data of I3d3/2 and I3d5/2 spectrum of WCND-DHFI. e, f) Deconvoluted data of N1s spectrum of WCND and WCND-DHFI.

a) Cell viability profile of WCND and WCND-DHFI at different concentrations (0.5–6 mg/mL) for 24 and 72 h. Showing a significant difference between groups in almost every concentration (**** P < 0.0001), also have shown a significant increase in viability after 72 h of incubation (***P value= 0.0002) b) Images portrayed the Chorioallantoic (CAM) membrane of a duck embryo treated with 4 mg/mL of WCND and WCND-DHFI for 0 h, 48 h, and 72 h.

Time-dependent bacteriostatic activity of WCND and WCND-DHFI a) against gram-positive bacteria, and b) against gram-positive bacteria, c-f Graph indicating the relation between bacterial cell viability and log reduction, representing the dose-dependent (0.8–4 mg/mL) bactericidal activity of WCND and WNCD-DHFI. c, d) Dose-dependent bacteriostatic activity of WCND and WCND-DHFI against gram-positive bacteria, c, d) Dose-dependent bacteriostatic activity of WCND and WCNDI against gram-negative bacteria.

Live/dead fluorescent staining images. S aureus and E coli cells were treated with WCND and WCND-DHFI and without samples (control) for 2 h. Representative fluorescence microscopy images of bacteria cells which were stained by SYTO 9 (green channel) and Live/dead fluorescent staining images. S aureus and E coli cells were treated with WCND and WCND-DHFI and without samples (control) for 2 h. Representative fluorescence microscopy images of bacteria cells which were stained by SYTO 9 (green channel) and PI (red channel) for 15 min of incubation in dark. All treated cases had an increasing dose of both samples from 0.8 mg/mL to 4 mg/mL. Scale bar = 10 µm.

a, d) SEM images of S. aureus and E. coli incubated without samples (scale- 1 µm); b, e) incubated with 4 mg/mL of WCND for 2 h c, f) incubated with 4 mg/mL of WCND-DHFI for 2 h. g-i) TEM micrographs of both strains, representing the morphologies with and without sample treatment (scale- 1 µm). Red arrows and circles indicate the disruption of bacterial cell walls after WCND and WCND-DHFI treatment.

a) Degree of bacterial membrane lipid oxidation assessed by the TBARS assay. The amount of MDA production through lipid peroxidation has enhanced significantly with increasing concentration of the sample (**** P < 0.0001). A significant difference between the two strains in terms of MDA production (** P = 0.0022). b) Gradual colour changes in the reaction solution due to an increment in MDA production. c) The degree of protein oxidation for bacteria exposed to WCND-DHFI. A significant difference was observed between all concentrations of samples (**** P < 0.0001). d) MDA standard curve. There was a significant difference between the two strains in terms of percentage of protein oxidation (* P = 0.0280).

head.ce:title: Iodine functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI) crosslinked whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND) for antibacterial application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering lab, School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering lab, School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering lab, School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nano Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Nano Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nano Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Punjab, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND) were synthesized using the microwave irradiation method, and its amine-rich surface functionality was crosslinked with covalently bound Iodine functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI) to produce WCND-DHFI. The physicochemical characterization of both WCND and WCND-DHFI was performed and compared to comprehend the consequence of iodination on the characteristics of WCND. The suitability of CND in biological environments was evaluated through in vitro cytocompatibility and Chorioallantoic Membrane (CAM) assay, as well as a hemocompatibility study. WCND-DHFI has shown enhanced cell viability against WCND. Further, the antibacterial properties of both CNDs were studied against both gram-positive and gram-negative bacterial strains, representing an enhancement in antibacterial activity after DHFI crosslinking. WCND-DHFI has depicted a stable and prominent bacteriostatic activity for up to 6 h for both strains of bacteria. WCND-DHFI has denoted a 99.996% and 99.999% loss of bacterial viability for gram-positive and negative strains, respectively. Novel surface functionalization portrays an improvement in antibacterial activity. Transmission and scanning electron microscopy represent the cell wall rupturing by the WCND-DHFI, resulting in bacterial death. The ROS-mediated bacteriostatic mechanism of WCND-DHFI has been explored through assessing lipid peroxidation and protein oxidation assay. Moreover, the oxidative damage of DNA also has been explored. WCND-DHFI is performing as a promising cytocompatible and hemocompatible material for antibacterial applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections have become a severe biomedical challenge causing increased death rates and morbidity. There is a contentious urge to develop alternative bacteriostatic strategies and bactericidal agents to prevent and treat infections to counter antimicrobial-resistant organisms [1]. Many researchers have investigated the mechanism of bactericidal activity of various metallic [2] and metal oxide-based [3,4] nanoparticles along with semiconductor and carbon nanodots (CND). CND provide promising avenues for bacterial deactivation due to their small size, surface charge, easy surface tunability, and good biocompatibility [5,6]. The possible mechanism involved in the bacteriostatic activity can be exhibited either by the robust photodynamic ROS production of CND [7] or by rupturing the bacterial cell wall by electrostatic interaction between charged surface groups and negatively charged bacterial membranes ( Fig. 1). CND can generate hydroxyl free radicals (OH•) and singlet oxygen (1O2) after interacting with bacterial cells, thereby destroying many of the critical biomolecules, and damaging DNA/RNA through inductive oxidative stress leading to cell death [8,9]. Over the last few years, researchers have explored the bacteriostatic activity of carbon nanodots after different surface functionalizations. Liu et al. have described the enhancement of ROS-mediated bacteriostatic activity through the conjugation of CDs with AuAg NCs to promote charge carrier separation via energy transfer. CDs@AuAg NCs have shown excellent antibacterial activity against both strains of bacteria with a 4–5 log kill value [10]. Another group of researchers has crosslinked CND with 2,20-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) as the surface functionalizing molecule, exhibiting good antimicrobial activities against both E. coli and Bacillus subtilis [11]. R. Jijie et al. have synthesized amine-terminated carbon dots (CDs-NH2) functionalized with ampicillin (AMP) generating a moderate amount of ROS under visible light effectively killing E. coli [12]. In another study scientists have done surface passivation of CND by colloidal TiO2, exhibiting a mechanism of photodynamic killing of bacteria [13].

The precursors for CND can be categorized as natural or synthetic. Natural precursors have the advantage of low prices, high abundance, low toxicity, easy formulation of functional groups for bio-conjugation, and eco-friendliness over synthetic carbon dot sources [14]. The use of green precursors also reduced the chance of cytotoxicity [15,16]. CND's easy functionalization, excellent water solubility, and significantly higher biocompatibility can be achieved and show a comparatively low cytotoxicity level [9]. The chemical, optical, and electrical properties of the CND nanostructure can be tuned either by doping in the core or functionalization on the shell/surface [17]. Surface functionalization is a multidimensional method that plays a vital role in research fields of biomedical applications [18]. In recent years, few researchers have studied the crosslinking effect of moieties by conjugation with biomolecules, which can be applied in multiple ranges [19]. Iodinated molecules are commonly used for several decades having broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, and a tenacious biofilm-penetrating bacteriostatic efficacy. However, according to some reports, researchers have raised concerns about the side effects of iodine over ineffective penetration, allergy, and toxic effects on host cells. Extended long-term use can provoke abnormal thyroid gland function, hepatic or renal insufficiencies, metabolic acidosis, convulsions, etc. [20] These consequences create a need for less toxic iodinated disinfectants, with effective broad-spectrum antibacterial activity.

Whey protein is a protein-rich cheese industry waste having milk components like alpha-lactoglobulin, beta-lactoglobulin, and casein including vital amino acids [21]. Researchers recently synthesized CND from an aqueous whey protein solution using single-step microwave-mediated processing. They have confirmed it as excellent biocompatibility, and a non-immunogenic tool for in vitro live cell imaging [22]. In this current research, we have synthesized whey protein-derived CND (WCND). As the significant components of whey protein are enriched with amino acids, more -NH2 groups will be available on the surface of the WCND, helping in crosslinking any compatible functional groups. One recent study has shown that DHF can be converted into butenedial through protolytic cleavage, having bifunctional dialdehyde could be iodinated to act as a cross-linker entity for free amine groups of silk molecules [23]. The carbonyl moiety of iodinated bifunctional butenedial could react with free amine groups through a Schiff base reaction. CN bond formed among silk fibroins via nucleophilic attack [23]. Researchers also have shown that the DHF can be used as a potential temperature-controlled cross-linker under acidic conditions for chitosan [24]. The free amine groups of WCND also might crosslink with the DHFI, forming the CN bond [25]. As far as we are aware, there is no significant research on any natural precursor-derived carbon dots crosslinked with any iodinated compound for broad-spectrum antibacterial application.

The current study used the microwave irradiation technique to synthesize whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND). As prepared, WCND was crosslinked with iodine-functionalized DHF to obtain DHFI-crosslinked WCND (WCND-DHFI). The physicochemical characterization of both WCND and WCND-DHFI was performed to appreciate the outcome of crosslinking on the properties of CND. The suitability of CND in a biological niche was assessed through cytocompatibility both in vitro and ex vivo models, as well as hemocompatibility. Furthermore, the broad-spectrum antibacterial efficacy of both CND was explored against E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains. The results from the antibacterial study represent improved cytocompatibility, and antibacterial activity in crosslinked WCND-DHFI compared to uncrosslinked WCND, which can be proposed for broad-spectrum antibacterial application.

Whey protein was procured from Myprotein, UK. Sigma-Aldrich provided the DHF (mixture of cis and trans −97%) (Sigma-D134104), resublimed Iodine, ninhydrin reagent, 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) salt, Trichloro acetic acid (TCA), and 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (2,4-DNPH). For cell culture studies, we obtained Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) high glucose and Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) from Gibco and an antibiotic solution from HiMedia. Luria Bertani (LB) Broth, Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA), and EZAssay™ TBARS Estimation Kit were procured from HiMedia for antibacterial research. The Live/Dead BacLight® Bacterial Viability Kit and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (H2DCFDA) was procured from Thermo Scientific. Deionized water (Milli-Q 18.2 MΩ.cm) was employed in all the reactions.

A 1% (wt./vol.) whey protein was dispersed in deionized (DI) water and kept in continuous stirring using a mechanical stirrer (REMI, India) at increased pH until it dissolved properly ( Fig. 2a). It was instantly shifted to a microwave (1000 W, 2.45 GHz). The irradiation time for WCND synthesis was adjusted via microwaving for diverse time intervals (3 min, 5 min, and 7 min) (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crosslinking of WCND with DHFI and ninhydrin assay
ce:section.ce:para:
As prepared, WCND with amine group was crosslinked with iodine functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI). Firstly, iodine-functionalized DHF was prepared by mixing 19.2 mM iodine solution (dissolved in 50 mL of 30% ethanol) with 38 mM DHF in a 1:1 ratio under constant stirring at 25 °C [24]. DHFI solution and WCND were mixed and heated at 60 °C for cross-linking. To determine the crosslinking density of WCND with DHFI, a ninhydrin (2, 2-Dihydroxyindane-1, 3-dione) assay was performed before mixing these together. In this assay, WCND samples with different concentrations of DHFI (10%, 25% & 50%) solution before and after crosslinking, were heated in acidic pH with 0.1% ninhydrin solution at 75 °C, and absorbance of the solution was measured at 575 nm with a well plate reader. Glycine was used as the standard at several identified concentrations. Using the following equation, crosslinking density was calculated.
(1)
% Crosslinking
density = [ (
C
b
−
C
a
)
/
C
b
] × 100
Cb and Ca are the absorbances of uncrosslinked and crosslinked samples at 575 nm.

The WCND and WCND-DHFI were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker India), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECHNAI G2), fluorescence spectroscopy (Varian Cary), UV-Visible spectroscopy (Shimadzu), Raman spectroscopy (Jobin Yvon Horiba), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Carl Zeiss, 30 kV), EDAX analysis (Oxford Instruments), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Perkin Elmer). A Malvern Zetasizer machine performed dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis and zeta potential measurements. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG scientific ESCALAB MK-2, Mg Kα) was also used to characterise iodine crosslinked WCND.
The detailed method has been mentioned in the ESI†.

The L929 cell line (Purchased from NCCS, Pune) was utilized for cytocompatibility testing. The 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]− 2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was performed to evaluate the percentage viability of cells incubating with and without CND. Before incubating with samples, approximately 5000 cells were seeded per well in a tissue culture well plate and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. A gradual sequence of dilutions (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 mg/mL) of WCND and WCND-DHFI was added to each well in quadruplets, and wells without cells were kept as blanks. The MTT reagent (0.5 mg/mL) was applied to every well after 24 h and 72 h of incubation. The purple formazan crystals formed in each well were dissolved using dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and the optical density (OD) of this liquid was examined at 570 nm using a well plate reader (Thermo Scientific Multiskan GO plate reader). The percentage of viability was calculated concerning the absorbance (OD) taken from the wells incubated without samples kept as control, using the following formula.
(2)
% Cell
viability =
(
D
t
−
D
b
)
/
(
D
c
−
D
b
)
× 100
Dt = OD of test samples,
DC = OD of controls, and.
DB = OD of Blanks.
Mentioned in detail in the ESI†.

The bacteria culture experiments used the E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains. Kinetic growth curves were prepared to assess the antibacterial activity of the CND. Firstly, the OD of the previously prepared bacterial culture was measured at 600 nm. Then the cell culture was diluted with an appropriate amount of LB until the OD was 0.1 A.U. The newly standardized diluted culture was incubated with optimized concentrations of WCND and WCND-DHFI from the cytocompatibility study, in an incubator shaker at 250 rpm at 37 °C for 6 h. The control tube also has been set without the sample in similar conditions. Initially, samples were taken every 30 min for the first 3 h. Then samples were taken at intervals of 1 h for the remaining 3 h. For all samples, OD at 600 nm was measured. The results were plotted against time (representing cell growth curves) correlated with the control (cells grown without sample).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria cultivation and loss of bacterial cell viability assessment
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, a bacterial pre-inoculum was prepared by adding a small number of bacteria from glycerol stock in 5 mL fresh LB, and the pre-inoculum was incubated at 37 °C overnight. The running culture was prepared by adding 100 μL of the pre-inoculum in 10 mL of LB, and the mixture was incubated in a shaker incubator at 250 rpm for 16 h at 37 °C [26].

Cells from bacterial cell culture were pelleted by centrifuging at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was transferred, and cell pellets were washed with isotonic saline solution and centrifuged under the above conditions. Washing was repeated two more times. Cell pellets were resuspended with isotonic saline solution. The colony forming unit (CFU) was calculated by counting bacterial colonies of that suspension after successive 10-fold dilutions and plating on MHA plates. The bacterial stock was diluted to make 106 CFU/mL per the requirement. Increasing concentrations (0.8, 1.6, 2.4, 3.2, and 4 mg/mL) of samples were added into tubes containing the diluted bacterial suspensions and incubated at 37 °C in an incubator shaker at 250 rpm. After incubation, 100 μL of bacterial suspension from every tube was spread on an MHA plate. Plates were kept to nurture at 37 °C. The colonies were counted and compared with the control sample. The log reduction values were calculated from each sample's measured bacterial load (CFU/mL).
(2)
Log
r
eduction
=
l
o
g
10
×
(
A
/
B
)
A= Initial bacterial load in CFU/mL,
B= Final bacterial load in CFU/mL after treatment.

The % of bacterial reduction has been calculated using the following formula.
(4)
%
o
f
b
a
c
t
e
r
i
a
l
r
e
d
u
c
t
i
o
n
=
100
− (
100
× (
C
F
U
/
m
L
S
÷
C
F
U
/
m
L
C
)
Whereas.
CFU/mLS = Colony counts for samples.
CFU/mLC = Colony counts for control.

Diluted 1 × 106 CFU/mL bacterial cell suspensions were obtained. In 1.5 mL microcentrifuge tubes, 900 mL of bacterium suspensions were mixed with 100 μL of samples in increasing concentrations (0.8, 1.6, 2.4, 3.2, and 4 mg/mL). Bacterial cell culture without sample kept as control. All tubes were incubated for 2 h at 37 °C in an incubator shaker at 250 rpm. The Live/Dead BacLight® Bacterial Viability Kits were used to validate the bacterial viability result. This kit contains two separate dyes, SYTO 9, and propidium iodide (PI). A green-fluorescent viable cells stain, and a red-fluorescent dead cell stain respectively. SYTO 9 and PI were added 1:1 to one clean microcentrifuge tube to make a dye mixture. After 2 h of incubation with samples, 1 mL of bacterial suspension was mixed with the 3 μL of dye mixture and incubated at room temperature in the dark for 15 min 5 μL of the stained bacterial suspension was trapped on an 18 mm square coverslip. We examined them in an Inverted Fluorescence Microscope (Axio Observer. Z1, Carl Zeiss). The wavelength for excitation/emission of two dyes was 493/505 − 520 nm for the SYTO 9 and 557/590 − 610 nm for PI.

The structural abnormalities of bacterial cells before and after incubating with samples were inspected by Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Supra 40, Carl Zeiss AG). The bacteria suspension containing 1 × 106 CFU/mL cells was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C in an incubator shaker at 250 rpm in the presence of samples having 4 mg/mL of concentration. After incubation, all sample incubated bacterial cells including controls, drop cast on coverslips and finally fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde. Followed by gold coating images were captured through SEM. The morphological deformities of bacterial cell walls were also examined through TEM micrographic analysis. TEM images were observed with a TECHNAI G2 transmission electron microscope. The samples were prepared by placing a drop of homogeneous bacterial suspension on a copper grid with a lacey carbon film, allowing it to dry in the air.

The dose-dependent effect of WCND-DHFI on ROS generation was evaluated at 2 h by flow cytometry using H2DCFDA as the oxidative probe. The lipid peroxidation (TBRAS) assay and protein oxidation assay have been performed as well as checked the oxidative damage of DNA in the presence of WCND-DHFI. The detailed procedure has been mentioned in the ESI†.

Whey protein is a processed milk industry-generated waste product, rich in globular proteins such as lactoglobulins and calcium caseinate. The precursor amino acid chains can be carbonized into ultra-small WCND by the stress induced by microwave irradiation (Fig. 2a). Gelation tendency also can be observed at increasing irradiation time. Therefore, time standardization was needed to find a stable gel-free WCND suspension. By changing the microwave irradiation time intervals, the perfect time was optimized for WCND synthesis. The optimization was validated via photographic inspection of CND as shown in Fig. 2b. After 7 min of microwaving, the sample showed some gelation (Fig. 2b); therefore, up to 5 min of microwaving would be ideal for CND preparation from whey protein. The unreacted large particles and agglomerates were filtered out by centrifugation for 10 min at 5000 rpm. Then we prepared a 20 mg/mL stock concentration by dispersing freeze-dried WCND powder in DI water.

The furan ring of DHF breaks into a bifunctional butenedial in an acidic environment by protolytic cleavage, indicated in Fig. 2c. The molecular iodine is attached through an electrophilic reaction on butenedial. Eventually, through the formation of a transitional iodonium ion, 2,3-diiodo butenedial was developed in acidic conditions. Iodine is represented as an electrophile (I+) in the occurrence of alkene-like Lewis bases [23]. The alkene (butenedial) possesses the π bonding electrons, approachable to Iodine (I2) has electrophilic nature, and removes free iodide (I−). Gradually, by forming a transitional bridged iodonium ion, iodine is added to the butenedial. Finally, the vicinal di-iodide was created via an opening of the iodonium ring through nucleophilic gegenion (I−), uncoupling the C−I bond. Through a Schiff base reaction, the carbonyl moiety of iodinated bifunctional butenedial could react with free amine groups available at the surface of WCND and might crosslink through CN bond formation via nucleophilic attack. Depending on the ninhydrin assay result, we have chosen the sample having 50% DHFI crosslinked with WCND for further characterization, as more than that may cause iodine-related cytotoxicity. The detailed analysis has been mentioned in Fig. S1 (ESI†).
The physicochemical properties of the synthesized WCND and WCND-DHFI were characterized as well as compared to further explore their multi-dimensional biomedical applications.

The TEM micrograph of the WCND and WCND-DHFI (Fig. S2, a, b, ESI†) indicates the occurrence of spherical morphology of nanoparticles for both CNDs. DLS result represents the hydrodynamic size of 2.3 and 3.2 nm for WCND and WCND-DHFI, respectively (Fig. S2 c, d, ESI†). WCND-DHFI have a zeta potential of − 7.1 mV fairly proving stable existence (Fig. 7 ESI†). The EDAX analysis has confirmed the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sodium, and sulphur in both WNCD and WCND-DHFI, while iodine was identified only in WCND-DHFI (Fig. S2 e-g, ESI†). The formation of the CN bond in the surface group can shift absorption peaks to longer wavelengths and change their absorption intensity [27]. The XRD pattern of both WCND and WCND-DHFI has shown an amorphous nature. The detailed analysis of XRD has been mentioned in Fig. S3 (ESI†).

The DHFI surface functionalization also has been confirmed by UV-Vis spectroscopy. A redshift of n-π * (∼350 nm) transition peak has appeared after iodine crosslinking due to the existence of CN groups along with the CO group. The detailed analysis of UV–vis spectroscopy has been mentioned in Fig. S4 (ESI†). The fluorescence spectroscopic analysis has depicted a blue shift accompanied by a declined intensity of emission maxima of WCND-DHFI with respect to WCND. This phenomenon ensures the fluorescence quenching effect due to DHFI surface functionalization around WCND. The detailed analysis of fluorescence spectroscopy has been mentioned in Fig. S5, ESI†.

The vibrational modes of the functional groups were analyzed through FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 3a). The FTIR spectrum of WCND has appeared with major peaks for C-H ethylenic stretch (633.9 cm−1), -CH3 bending vibration (1392.9 cm−1), N-H stretch for primary amines (3261.6 cm−1) -OH stretching (3443.5 cm−1), and a broad peak representing superimposition of CC stretch and amine specific N-H bending (1634.9 cm−1) [22]. The spectrum of DHFI has confirmed the 2-butenedial formation by the appearance of aldehydic =CH stretch (2978.4 cm−1) and a range of peaks (1720–1640 cm−1) representing conjugated and unconjugated -CO groups of aldehydes [28]. A significant decrease of intensity in this aldehydic -CO region was observed for WCND-DHFI along with a new peak at 1716.3 cm−1 representing imine (-CN) stretch, further validated by Raman and XPS analysis. Crosslinking of the aldehydic -CO group of DHFI with NH2 groups of WCND will form an imine group (CN) via Schiff base reaction [24]. This phenomenon has also been supported by the reduction in the intensity of the N-H stretch peak of primary amines (3251.6 cm−1) after DHFI crosslinking. The presence of iodine was observed in both DHFI and WCND-DHFI. In the spectrum of WCND-DHFI, iodine crosslinking has been established by the occurrence of C-I stretches (in the range 400–500 cm−1).

The data outcomes from Raman spectroscopy were also helpful in corroborating the CND synthesis and appearance of their surface functional groups (Fig. 3b). Two significant peaks for graphite-like carbon structures the D and G bands were detected in both of the samples at around 1410 cm−1 and 1550 cm−1, respectively. Laterally, a broad peak was observed around 1290 cm−1 for CS proving the presence of sulphur in whey protein. The presence of a new intensified peak around 1710 cm−1 proves the formation of the CN linkage. This can be reflected as an indication of the integration of DHFI crosslinking with the carbon backbone.

The XPS spectrum represented in Fig. 3c discloses the occurrence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, Sulphur, and sodium encompassing functional groups both in WCND and WCND-DHFI, whereas some extra peaks for iodine-containing groups have been identified in WCND-DHFI. In the deconvoluted C1s spectrum of the WCND, two components were identified (Fig. S6 a-b, ESI†), the peak centred at ∼284.13 eV was for sp2, and sp3 hybridized graphitic carbon atom (CC/C-C) [29], and a peak at ∼286.4 eV was accredited to the carbon in amine groups forming C–N bonds. Whereas for WCND-DHFI along with the peak related to the C-C bond, a new prominent peak at ∼287.78 eV has appeared due to R-HCN bond formation [29]. The DHFI crosslinking also has been confirmed by the N1s spectrum of WCND-DHFI (Fig. 3e, f), exhibiting a new peak at ∼398.5 eV, which is assigned as the CN bond, and the appearance of a suppressed peak of the -NH2 bond (∼399.95 eV) of amines [30]. The deconvoluted I3d3/2 and I3d5/2 peaks of WCND-DHFI (Fig. 3d) have shown prominent peaks attributed to I2 (∼630.71, and 619.15 eV) and I3 - (∼628.63, and 617.25 eV) validating the DHFI crosslinking with WCND [31].

After performing a hemocompatibility assay we have found that up to 6 mg/mL of concentration, WCND-DHFI has shown hemocompatibility. The detailed analysis of the hemocompatibility assay has been mentioned in Fig. S7 (ESI†).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and CAM assay
ce:section.ce:para: Both WCND and WCND-DHFI have exhibited good cytocompatibility up to 4 mg/mL of concentration in the L929 fibroblast cell line ( Fig. 4a). Also 4 mg/mL of WCND-DHFI have no toxic effect on duck egg embryos even after 72 h incubation (Fig. 4b). The detailed analysis of the cytocompatibility assay and CAM assay has been mentioned in ESI†. Depending on these results, we have used the 4 mg/mL of both samples for further experiments.

To evaluate the antibacterial action of the CND, the OD at 600 nm was documented at 30 min interludes up to 3 h, during the log phase of bacterial growth, in the presence of CND. The outcomes are plotted as growth curves and evaluated against the control culture, incubated in the absence of CND ( Fig. 5a, b). The concentration of 4 mg/mL of WCND and WCND-DHFI have shown bacteriostatic activity in the initial 30 min for both E. coli and S. aureus bacterial strains. WCND revealed better bacteriostatic activity against the gram-negative strain than the gram-positive strain. The WCND-DHFI has depicted a stable and prominent bacteriostatic activity even up to 2 h for both strains of bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loss of Bacterial cell viability assessment
ce:section.ce:para: To acquire an improved perception of the bacteriostatic activity of the CND, observed their concentration-dependent bactericidal properties. We incubated five varied dosages (0.8, 1.6, 2.4, 3.2, 4 mg/mL) of WCND and WCND-DHFI with 106 CFU/mL stock of both gram-positive and gram-negative strains of bacteria for 2 h, time optimized from the previous time-dependent study. The lowest concentration of WCND has shown a 2.8 log reduction for gram-positive bacteria, and the highest concentration has shown ∼4.1 log reduction (Figs. 5c, 5d). WCND-DHFI depicted a better bacteriostatic activity against gram-positive bacteria, showing ∼3 log reduction at 0.8 mg/mL concentration and ∼4.2 log reduction at 4 mg/mL concentration (Figs. 5e, 5 f). In comparison to the gram-positive strain, both WCND and WCND-DHFI have revealed better antibacterial activity against the gram-negative strain, executing ∼5 log reduction. The WCND has represented a 99.95% and 99.999% drop in bacterial cell viability for gram-positive and negative strains, respectively. Whereas WCND-DHFI has denoted a 99.996% and 99.999% loss in bacterial cell viability for both strains, respectively.

The bacterial live/dead staining was performed and detected with a fluorescence microscope to identify the viability status of bacteria cells. The gram-positive and gram-negative strains of bacterial suspensions containing 1 × 106 CFU/mL cells were used. Then incubated with 0.8, 1.6, 2.4, 3.2, and 4 mg/mL of samples at 37 °C for 2 h. After incubating with a 1:1 mixture of the SYTO 9 and propidium iodide stains, live bacteria with uninterrupted cell membranes stain green, whereas dead bacteria with disrupted membranes take red stain. According to the figures obtained, standardized planktonic bacterial suspension was killed after treatment. Referred to Fig. 6, after treatment with samples bacteria cells were damaged higher than the control. This result means the dose-dependent killing of both strains of bacteria.

To investigate the bacteriostatic mechanism and faith of bacterial morphology after incubating with samples, we performed SEM. As shown in Fig. 7a-f, S. aureus and E. coli without samples are regular in exterior construct, unruptured surface, and structurally undamaged. Gradually, the bacteria cells become contracted and lose their membrane integrity after the treatment of 4 mg/mL of WCND and WCND-DHFI, which validates the viability data mentioned in the above tests. For both gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, similar phenomena were detected. The surface structures of both the control and the treated bacterial cells were examined using TEM. Both the WCND and WCND-DHFI-treated bacterial cells were significantly changed, and major damage was observed in the outer membrane (Fig. 7g-i).

Protein oxidation and lipid peroxidation can be attained via ROS-mediated damage mechanisms, resulting in bacterial cell death. WCND-DHFI has shown ROS generation in flow cytometry data (Fig.S9 ESI†) as well as a significant amount of lipid peroxidation and protein oxidation ( Fig. 8). The evidence of oxidative damage to DNA was provided by assessing the electrophoretic mobility of plain DNA and fragmented DNA (Fig.S10 ESI†). The detailed analysis of the bacteriostatic mechanism has been mentioned in ESI†.

The microwave irradiation technique synthesized whey protein-derived carbon nanodots (WCND), and its amine-rich surface group was crosslinked with Iodine-functionalized 2,5-dimethoxy-2,5-dihydrofuran (DHFI) to produce WCND-DHFI. The physicochemical characterization of both WCND and WCND-DHFI were compared to recognize the effect of crosslinking on the properties of CND. The studies show the formation of spherical CND with a hydrodynamic diameter of less than 5 nm. The ninhydrin assay is used to evaluate the crosslinking density of DHFI with an amine group of WCND. The chemical characterization confirms the presence of functionalized iodine on the DHF surface further confirming the cross-linking. The suitability of CND in a biological environment was evaluated using cytocompatibility, hemocompatibility and CAM assay results which represent lower toxicity after cross-linking. Further, the antibacterial properties of both CND were studied and compared, representing an improvement in antibacterial activity after cross-linking. The WCND exhibited better bacteriostatic activity against the gram-negative strain than the gram-positive strain while WCND-DHFI has depicted a stable and prominent bacteriostatic activity even up to 6 h for both strains of bacteria. The WCND has represented 99.95% and 99.999% reduction in bacterial cell viability for gram-positive and negative strains respectively. Whereas, WCND-DHFI has denoted a 99.996% and 99.999% reduction in bacterial cell viability for both strains, respectively. The ROS-mediated bacterial killing of the sample has been assessed through examining oxidative damage of cellular lipid, protein and genetic material. WCND-DHFI can be used as a cytocompatible and hemocompatible bacteriostatic nanomaterial for biomedical applications.

Sayan Mukherjee designed and performed the experiments analyzed the results and drafted the manuscript. Anurag Kumar Pandey contributed to performing the experiments and analysing the results. Nantu Dogra performed and analyzed some of the experiments. Uday Kumar Singh contributed to performing the molecular biology experiments. Bodhisatwa Das and Santanu Dhara revised the manuscript and approved the submitted version. Santanu Dhara contributed to the supervision, and acquisition of resources and management.

Sayan Mukherjee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Anurag Kumar Pandey: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Nantu Dogra: Validation, Formal analysis. Uday Kumar Singh: Formal analysis. Bodhisatwa Das: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Santanu Dhara: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their gratitude to Central Research Facilities (CRF), IIT Kharagpur for providing the material characterizing facilities. The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. Soumen Das, Prof. Nilmani Sarkar, Dr. Gayatri Mukherjee, and Dr. Ritobrata Goswami for using their facilities. The authors would like to thank Trina Roy, Ragavi Rajasekaran, Atul Kumar Ojha, Krishnabrata Panda, Suman Mishra, Biswajit Biswas, Shagnik Chatterjee, Saikat Biswas, and Debangshu Mukherjee for their valuable input and support throughout the experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113543.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
